title: How to Digest Data and Develop Treatment Recommendations During a Clinic Visit

items.title: Why postmeal glucose remains difficult on hybrid closed-loop pumps
items.text: Despite improved overall glycemic control, regulating postmeal glucose is still challenging because carbohydrate counting is imperfect for mixed meals and the patient—not the algorithm—still determines meal boluses.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "With the use of hybrid closed-loop pumps, despite the improvement in glycemic control, regulating postmeal glucose concentrations is still a challenge."

items.title: How to use time in range when interpreting pump downloads
items.text: Target time in range >70%, time below range <4%, and time above range <25% for most adults; fourteen days of CGM data correlate well with hemoglobin A1c and respond faster to regimen changes than A1c alone.
items.reference: Usual Target Recommendations: "Time in range >70%"

items.title: Why glucose management indicator may differ from hemoglobin A1c
items.text: When GMI is lower than A1c, consider avoiding hypoglycemia or raising the A1c target; when GMI is higher, address hyperglycemia. Discordance may reflect A1c interference, recent glycemic change, or calibration issues.
items.reference: Using GMI: "If GMI is lower than hemoglobin A1c considers Avoiding hypoglycemia"

items.title: How to choose a CGM when hypoglycemia unawareness is present
items.text: Avoid the original Libre without alerts; DexCom, Guardian 3, and Eversense all work, with DexCom favored when calibration error is a concern. For pump/closed-loop goals, match sensor to pump partner (Guardian 3 for Medtronic, DexCom for Tandem).
items.reference: Choosing a Sensor: "Not Libre (no alerts), but consider Libre 2, which does have alarms"

items.title: DexCom G6: factory calibration and alert capabilities
items.text: G6 is factory calibrated with MARD <10%, 10-day wear, high/low and predictive alerts, and no routine need for fingerstick confirmation—though compression artifact and site rotation remain practical limits.
items.reference: DexCom: "No need for fingerstick confirmation"

items.title: How closed-loop systems have progressed over two decades
items.text: Systems evolved from threshold suspend (pump off at low glucose), to predictive low-glucose suspend, to hybrid closed-loop where the algorithm corrects toward target while the patient still delivers meal boluses.
items.reference: Sensor-Pump Interactions: Toward "Closing the Loop": "Hybrid closed-loop systems: the algorithm corrects the glucose toward a target/target range; the patient still determines meal boluses"

items.title: How late meal boluses cause postprandial variability on downloads
items.text: When glucose rises before the bolus is delivered, early postprandial insulin is inadequate and later periods have relative insulin excess—producing late hypoglycemia even on hybrid closed-loop systems.
items.reference: Case 2: "the blood glucose levels start to rise BEFORE the meal bolus delivery."

items.title: Why manual correction boluses on closed-loop cause hypoglycemia
items.text: When the closed-loop system is already lowering glucose, patient-initiated correction boluses stack insulin and provoke hypoglycemia at variable times—exercise-induced lows are less likely when lows follow glucose spikes.
items.reference: Case 3: "she is in effect inadvertently "stacking" insulin and overcorrecting (Answer A)."

items.title: Pregnancy CGM glucose targets on pump/sensor downloads
items.text: Pregnancy targets for time in range are 63 to 140 mg/dL (3.5–7.8 mmol/L)—looser than nonpregnant targets in older patients but tighter overnight control may still require insulin timing adjustments.
items.reference: Usual Target Recommendations: "Pregnancy targets for time in range are 63 to 140 mg/dL (3.5-7.8 mmol/L)."

items.title: Pitfall: nocturnal false lows from compression artifact
items.text: DexCom and other sensors can report "compression lows" when the sensor is compressed during sleep; unconfirmed lows with discordant fingersticks should prompt site/position counseling—not forced recalibration of factory-calibrated systems.
items.reference: DexCom: ""Compression lows""

items.title: When to consider implantable Eversense CGM
items.text: Eversense suits body-image or athletic concerns, adherence problems, needle phobia, or running out of infusion/sensor sites—but it lacks a current US pump partner for threshold suspend or hybrid closed loop.
items.reference: So When Eversense?: "Body image issues/athletics"

items.title: How to handle pump/CGM downloads during a busy visit
items.text: Efficient interpretation requires pre-visit upload, focus on time in range, GMI, auto-mode utilization, bolus timing relative to meals, and correction-bolus patterns—technology choice must align with insurance, alerts, and pump integration needs.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Logistics of office download and interpretation of data from pump/CGM and closed-loop systems."

items.question: A 35-year-old pregnant woman with type 2 diabetes on bedtime NPH has elevated postlunch glucose on Libre download; overnight values are low-normal. Time in range is 88%. What is the best next step?
options: Continue the regimen; time in range is 88%
Reduce NPH because time below range is 9%
Move NPH insulin to the morning
items.explanation: Pregnancy target is 63–140 mg/dL; postlunch hyperglycemia with acceptable overnight values favors morning NPH to cover daytime meals rather than reducing bedtime NPH or adding metformin.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: C) Move her NPH insulin to the morning"

items.question: A 46-year-old man on a 670G hybrid closed-loop has A1c 8.1% with wide variability. Pump download shows glucose rising before meal bolus delivery. Most likely explanation?
items.explanation: Rising glucose before bolus delivery causes early under-insulinization and later relative excess—classic late bolus pattern on CGM/pump overlay.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: D) Late meal boluses"

items.question: A 51-year-old woman on 670G auto-mode has postprandial spikes followed by hypoglycemia at variable times. She takes manual correction boluses when high. Most likely cause of lows?
items.explanation: Hypoglycemia follows spikes and coincides with manual corrections while auto-mode is already delivering insulin—stacking effect.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: A) Overcorrecting her blood glucose"

items.question: A 60-year-old man with LADA on DexCom G6 has recurrent ~3 AM sensor lows unconfirmed by fingerstick; recalibrating with fingersticks worsened accuracy. Best management?
options: Counsel on compression artifact and avoid unnecessary G6 recalibration
Switch to Guardian 3 requiring daily calibration
items.explanation: Factory-calibrated G6 lows discordant with fingersticks during sleep suggest compression lows; forced recalibration can worsen accuracy.
items.reference: Case 4: "He used fingerstick blood glucose to recalibrate his sensor, with even worsening accuracy."

items.subtopic: Sensor choice — type 2 without hypo unawareness
items.question: Which CGM is often favored for a patient with type 2 diabetes and no hypoglycemia unawareness when cost and ease matter?
options: No CGM is appropriate in type 2 diabetes
items.explanation: All four sensors can work; Libre may be easier, less costly, and at least as accurate as fingersticks with factory calibration.
items.reference: Choosing a Sensor: "Libre may be easier, less costly, and at least as accurate as fingerstick measurements and is factory calibrated"

items.question: A patient desires Tandem Control-IQ hybrid closed-loop. Which sensor partner is appropriate?
items.explanation: Tandem pairs with DexCom; Medtronic systems use Guardian 3.
items.reference: Choosing a Sensor: "If desire Tandem System (vide infra) is desired, then DexCom"

items.question: How many days of CGM data generally suffice to correlate with hemoglobin A1c?
items.explanation: Fourteen days of CGM correlate well with A1c for clinic interpretation.
items.reference: Regarding Time in Range: "Fourteen days suffice to correlate well with hemoglobin A1c."

items.question: Per the chapter GMI formula, a 25 mg/dL increase in mean CGM glucose changes GMI by approximately:
items.explanation: GMI (%) = 3.31 + 0.02392 × mean CGM glucose; each 25 mg/dL raises GMI ~0.6%.
items.reference: Glucose Management Indicator: "Each change of 25 mg/dL results in a GMI increase of ~0.6%."

items.question: What does a low-glucose threshold suspend system do?
options: Shuts off insulin delivery when glucose falls below a preset threshold
Replaces all basal insulin with glucagon
Eliminates need for patient meal boluses
items.explanation: Threshold suspend stops the pump at a low glucose value—earliest step toward closed loop.
items.reference: Sensor-Pump Interactions: Toward "Closing the Loop": "Low-glucose (threshold) suspend systems: the pump shuts off if a low glucose value is used"

items.question: A patient with severe hypoglycemia unawareness needs alerts. Which option is inappropriate as sole CGM?
options: Original US Freestyle Libre without alarms
items.explanation: Original Libre lacks alerts; Libre 2 adds alarms. DexCom, Guardian, and Eversense provide alerts.
items.reference: Choosing a Sensor: "Not Libre (no alerts), but consider Libre 2, which does have alarms"

items.question: Why is Eversense generally avoided when threshold suspend or hybrid closed loop is desired?
options: It has no current US pump partner
items.explanation: Eversense lacks a pump partner for automated insulin delivery systems in the US at the time of the chapter.
items.reference: Choosing a Sensor: "Eversense does not have a "pump partner" at present, so would not use if threshold suspend or hybrid closed loop are desired"

items.question: Before optimizing bolus type or timing, what must first be verified on pump download review?
options: Patient uses only rapid-acting analog at all meals
items.explanation: Correct basal rates are foundational before adjusting bolus mode (regular, square-wave, combo).
items.reference: Successful Meal Bolus: "Ensure the basal rate(s) is/are correct"

items.question: Intensive glycemic control in the DCCT reduced microvascular complications but also caused what important adverse effect?
options: Loss of counterregulatory hormone response only in type 2 diabetes
items.explanation: DCCT intensive therapy tripled severe hypoglycemia—motivating safer intensification with pumps and RT-CGM.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "intensive diabetes control resulted in a tripling of the occurrence of severe hypoglycemia."

items.statement: On hybrid closed-loop systems the algorithm fully automates all meal boluses without patient input.
items.explanation: Hybrid closed-loop corrects toward target but the patient still determines meal boluses.
items.reference: Sensor-Pump Interactions: Toward "Closing the Loop": "the patient still determines meal boluses"

items.statement: A typical adult CGM target is time in range greater than 70%.
items.explanation: Usual recommendation is TIR >70% with TBR <4% and TAR <25%.
items.reference: Usual Target Recommendations: "Time in range >70%"

items.statement: DexCom G6 generally requires routine fingerstick calibration every 12 hours.
items.explanation: G6 is factory calibrated and does not require routine fingerstick confirmation.
items.reference: DexCom: "No need for fingerstick confirmation"

items.statement: Glucose management indicator (GMI) estimates hemoglobin A1c from mean CGM glucose.
items.explanation: GMI replaces the older eA1c terminology to estimate A1c from CGM mean glucose.
items.reference: Glucose Management Indicator: "GMI is a new term for estimating hemoglobin A1c from CGM readings"

items.statement: The older US Freestyle Libre version lacked alerts, unlike Libre 2.
items.explanation: Original Libre required active scanning and lacked alerts; Libre 2 adds optional alarms.
items.reference: Freestyle Libre: Flash Technology: "Older US version does not have alerts; Libre 2 has optional alarms"

items.statement: Eversense sensor life is approximately 3 months in the United States.
items.explanation: Implanted sensor lasts 3 months in the US and 6 months in the EU per chapter.
items.reference: Eversense (Senseonics): "Sensor life = 3 months United States; 6 months European Union"

items.statement: Time in range responds faster to therapeutic interventions than hemoglobin A1c.
items.explanation: TIR reflects recent glycemia and variability more rapidly than A1c.
items.reference: Advantages of Time in Range: "It responds faster to interventions than does hemoglobin A1c"

items.statement: At the time of writing, Medicare covered only Freestyle Libre and Dexcom among CGM systems.
items.explanation: Insurance coverage strongly influences sensor choice; Medicare coverage was limited to Libre and Dexcom.
items.reference: Selecting a Pump: "Medicare is only "covering" Freestyle Libre and Dexcom."

items.assertion: Assertion: Late meal boluses can cause late postprandial hypoglycemia on hybrid closed-loop therapy.
items.reason: Reason: Glucose begins rising before insulin is delivered, leaving early hyperglycemia and later relative insulin excess.
items.explanation: Both are true and mechanistically linked on pump/CGM downloads.
items.reference: Case 2: "there is inadequate insulin early after meals and excess insulin in the later postprandial period, resulting in late hypoglycemia."

items.assertion: Assertion: Manual correction boluses during auto-mode can provoke hypoglycemia.
items.reason: Reason: The closed-loop algorithm never delivers insulin when glucose is elevated.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; the algorithm does deliver insulin—stacking with patient corrections causes lows.
items.reference: Case 3: "she is taking "correction boluses" at a time that the closed-loop system is already correcting her blood glucose."

items.assertion: Assertion: Fourteen days of CGM data correlate well with hemoglobin A1c.
items.reason: Reason: Time in range is unaffected by red-cell turnover that alters A1c.
items.explanation: Both are true; TIR avoids some A1c confounders while correlating over ~14 days.
items.reference: Regarding Time in Range: "Fourteen days suffice to correlate well with hemoglobin A1c."

items.assertion: Assertion: Sensor choice may dictate pump choice for closed-loop systems.
items.reason: Reason: All sensors interoperate with all pumps without restriction.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; pump-sensor partnerships are proprietary—the reason is false.
items.reference: Selecting a Pump: "If a sensor-augmented system or closed-loop system will be used, the sensor may dictate choice of pump"

items.assertion: Assertion: Moving NPH from bedtime to morning may improve postlunch glucose in pregnancy.
items.reason: Reason: Bedtime NPH peaks during overnight hours when fasting targets are already acceptable.
items.explanation: Both are true for the case vignette physiology.
items.reference: Case 1: "Moving her NPH insulin to the morning (Answer C) is therefore the best next step."

items.assertion: Assertion: Original Freestyle Libre without alerts is unsuitable for severe hypoglycemia unawareness.
items.reason: Reason: Libre sensors are inherently inaccurate compared with fingersticks.
items.explanation: Both statements have support, but the lack of alerts—not accuracy—is the primary contraindication cited.
items.reference: Choosing a Sensor: "Not Libre (no alerts), but consider Libre 2, which does have alarms"

items.assertion: Assertion: When GMI exceeds hemoglobin A1c, intensifying hyperglycemia treatment should be considered.
items.reason: Reason: A higher GMI always proves laboratory A1c error.
items.explanation: Assertion reflects chapter guidance; GMI above A1c suggests hyperglycemia but does not prove lab error alone.
items.reference: Using GMI: "If GMI is higher than hemoglobin A1c consider: Addressing hyperglycemia"

items.assertion: Assertion: RT-CGM and pump advances aim to intensify control while reducing hypoglycemia.
items.reason: Reason: Intensive control in the DCCT carried no hypoglycemia penalty.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; DCCT intensive therapy tripled severe hypoglycemia—the reason is false.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "intensive diabetes control resulted in a tripling of the occurrence of severe hypoglycemia."
